segments.s012-g9: ز + noun + verb + تا + noun: ز (from) introduces the span/source; خاور (east) is the starting point; بیاراست is a past/preterite verb 'adorned' (3rd sg) — classical/poetic ordering places the verb after the object; تا ... باختر means 'as far as / to the west' (تا here = 'as far as'). The whole phrase = 'he adorned from the east as far as the west.'
segments.s012-g10: پدید آمد is a two-part verbal idea 'appeared / came into view' (پدید = 'appear', آمد = 'came'); از introduces the source 'from/out of'; فر here denotes the kingly/divine glory (the specialized meaning 'farr'); او is the possessive pronoun 'his'; کان is an archaic noun meaning 'a mine / vein (of ore)' and modifies زر 'gold' — so syntactically 'from his farr a vein of gold appeared.'
segments.s012-g11: مرا اختر is a possessive+noun construction in poetic order: 'مرا' = 'my' (preposed here, classical flexibility) modifying اختر 'star'. خفته is a past participle 'having been asleep' functioning as an adjective, and بیدار گشت is a compound past meaning 'became awake' (گشت = 'became/turned'). The clause therefore reads 'my star, (though) asleep, became awake.'
segments.s012-g12: به مغز اندر اندیشه is a locative/goal phrase: به ... اندر = 'into / within the mind'; مغز here = 'mind/brain' and اندیشه = 'thought'. بسیار modifies the verb as an adverb 'greatly / deeply'. گشت again is 'became / turned' so the clause means '(it) turned into deep thought within the mind.'
segments.s012-g13: بدانستم is simple past 'I knew' (1st sg). آمد (here) means 'came' and takes زمان سخن as its subject/object: 'the time of speech came.' Word order is flexible/poetic: verb may appear before or after its noun phrases but the sense is 'I perceived that the time for speaking had arrived.'
segments.s012-g14: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb) begins the clause; نو شود is a present/subjunctive-type form 'becomes / will become' (شود = 'become'); روزگار کهن = 'the old/ancient time/age' (روزگار modified by کهن). Poetic word order places the predicate 'نو شود' before its nominal subject. The sense: 'now the old age becomes (is made) new.'
segments.s012-g15: بر اندیشه is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the thought (of...)'; شهریار زمین is a genitival phrase 'the sovereign/king of the earth' (شهریار = king, زمین = earth). The whole segment sets the circumstance: 'on the thought of the king of the earth.'
segments.s012-g16: بخفتم is 1st person past 'I slept / I lay' (ب‑ prefix appears in classical poetic past forms); شبی 'one night' gives the duration; لب پر از آفرین is a descriptive noun phrase 'lips full of praise' where پر از = 'full of' and آفرین = an exclamation/noun 'praise/acclamation.' Together: 'I slept one night, my lips full of praise.'
segments.s012-g1: This is a temporal clause headed by the noun 'جهان‌آفرین' (the World‑creator) followed by 'تا' used temporally as 'until'. The direct object 'جهان' comes before the past tense verb 'آفرید' (created), a common classical word order (subject + temporal marker + object + verb).
segments.s012-g2: 'چون' introduces the following clause with a temporal/causal sense ('since/when'); 'او' refers back to the creator. The verbal phrase 'نیامد پدید' is an inverted classical sequence meaning 'did not come into being/appear' (literally 'did not come — appear'), so the negated past verb 'نیامد' + predicate 'پدید' yields 'did not appear'.
segments.s012-g3: 'چو' here is a temporal conjunction 'when'; the phrase 'خورشید بر چرخ بنمود تاج' literally places the locative phrase 'بر چرخ' ('on the sky/heaven') before the verb 'بنمود' ('displayed/showed') with the object 'تاج' ('crown') ending the clause — classical verb‑final ordering for a transitive clause.
segments.s012-g4: This is a result clause with the copular verb 'شد' ('became'). 'به کردار' marks manner ('in the form/appearance of') and governs the following descriptive phrase 'تابنده عاج' ('shining ivory'), where the adjective 'تابنده' post‑modifies the noun 'عاج'. The overall meaning is 'the earth became in the manner of shining ivory.'
segments.s012-g5: 'چه گویم که' is a rhetorical formula 'what shall I say that...' where 'گویم' is a first‑person present/subjunctive form meaning 'shall I say / I can say' (a set rhetorical phrasing). The first 'که' introduces the subordinate clause ('that/who'), and the relative 'که بود' ('who was') uses the past copula 'بود' to set up the description of the sun.
segments.s012-g6: 'کز' is the classical contracted form of 'که از' and here means 'from whom' (source). 'او' (he) is the subject of the past verb 'فزود' ('increased' / 'grew'), 'در جهان' is a locative ('in the world'), and 'روشنایی' is the object ('light/brightness') — literally: 'from whom light in the world increased.'
segments.s012-g7: A nominal phrase naming the ruler: 'ابوالقاسم' is a proper name/kunya, followed by epithets 'آن شاه' ('that king') and 'پیروز بخت' ('victorious/fortunate'). The adjectives are appositional epithets describing the king.
segments.s012-g8: The verb 'نهاد' is the past tense of 'نهادن' ('placed/put') and governs the object 'تخت' (throne) at the clause end. The prepositional cluster 'از بر تاج خورشید' is a classical combination meaning 'upon/on the crown of the sun' (literally 'from/on crown sun'), so the whole phrase means 'he placed a throne upon the sun's crown.'
segments.s012-g17: This noun phrase means “my heart like a light in that dark night.” دلِ من is a possessed noun (دل + enclitic من = “my heart”); چو introduces a simile (“like/as”), followed by نور as the thing it is likened to. اندر آن تیره شب is a prepositional phrase (“in that dark night”) modifying the simile; the whole phrase sets up an image rather than carrying an explicit verb (the predicate comes after).
segments.s012-g18: This cluster gives the state of the heart introduced before. نخفته is an adjectival/predicative form meaning “unslept / not sleeping” (no copula is needed in Persian poetry). گشاده دل is a reversed, poetic adjective+noun order meaning “open‑hearted” (normally دل گشاده), and بسته لب similarly means “lips shut/closed”; و links the two coordinated descriptions.
segments.s012-g19: چنان is an adverb (“thus/so”) modifying the verb دید (past tense “saw”). روشن روانم is the subject phrase (“my bright/enlightened soul”), where -م is the possessive clitic “my.” به خواب here marks the mode/location of perception (“in a dream / in sleep”); the construction corresponds to English “(my soul) saw in a dream.”
segments.s012-g20: که introduces the reported content (“that …”). رخشنده شمعی is a descriptive noun phrase (“a radiant/gleaming candle”), with the adjective before the noun for poetic effect. بر آمد is a compound verbal idea meaning “rose up / came up” (بر indicating upward motion + آمد past of آمدن). ز آب is the origin phrase “from (the) water,” using the poetic preposition ز (= از).
segments.s012-g21: همه روی گیتی is a topical noun phrase “the entire face/surface of the world.” شب لاژورد is the predicate nominal “an ultramarine (lapis‑lazuli) night.” The copular/verb is not inside this short segment but is supplied by the following clause, a common poetic coordination where the state is asserted across the line break.
segments.s012-g22: از آن شمع marks cause/origin (“from that candle”). گشتی here functions as the past verb “became / was turned” (classical poetic past), linking the previous predicate to the simile that follows. چو یاقوت زرد is a simile “like a yellow ruby,” describing the color/appearance produced by the candle. (Note: گشتی is an older/poetic past form of گشتن “become/turn”; see commentary on historical morphology.)
segments.s012-g23: در و دشت is a paired noun phrase meaning “court (palace) and plain / town and field” (a classical contrast of city/court vs. country). بر سان دیبا is a simile phrase “as/like brocade” (بر سان = like/as, دیبا = brocade), and شدی is the past verb “became” (poetic past of شدن), so the whole segment states that town and plain became like brocade.
segments.s012-g24: یکی is an indefinite numeral “one/a certain.” تخت پیروزه is the noun phrase “a turquoise throne” (پیروزه = turquoise as color/stone). پیدا شدی is the poetic past equivalent of پیدا شد (“appeared / was made visible”); together the segment states that one turquoise throne became visible/appeared.
segments.s012-g33: چو is the temporal subordinating word 'when'. The noun phrase آن چهره خسروی ('that/ the royal face') precedes the verb as in Persian SOV order. دیدمی is an archaic past first-person form meaning 'I saw' (older variant of دیدم) and closes the clause.
segments.s012-g34: از marks the source/agent of the questioning ('from / of those men'). آن نامداران is 'those renownèd ones'. بپرسیدمی is an older first‑person past form meaning 'I asked' (ب‑ prefix + پرسید + ـمی); here it links as the main verb of the second clause and picks up the preceding temporal clause.
segments.s012-g35: که introduces an indirect question or doubt ('whether/that which'). این چرخ و ماه is a poetic comparison ('this [is like] the sky and the moon'). است is the copula 'is', and یا introduces the alternative 'or'. تاج و گاه pairs 'crown' with 'گاه' meaning 'time/occasion'.
segments.s012-g36: ستاره است is 'it is a star' (or 'a star is [there]'). پیش + اندر + ش form a small phrase 'پیش اندرش' = 'before him / in front of him' where اندر is an archaic locative/prepositional element and ش is the possessive clitic 'his'. یا سپاه gives the alternative 'or an army'.
segments.s012-g37: یکی گفت is the formula 'someone/a certain one said' (گفت here functions as 'said' though the provided lemma senses did not list the verbal sense). کاین introduces the reported clause ('that this ...'). شاه روم است و هند reads as a terse listing of domains: 'this king is [ruler] of Rome and Hind' with است as the copula joining subject and predicate.
segments.s012-g38: ز ... تا is a range expression 'from X to Y' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). قنوج is the place Kannauj; تا + پیش دریای سند gives the end point 'as far as before the Sea of Sind' (پیش = 'before, up to').
segments.s012-g39: به ایران و توران gives the territorial scope 'in / concerning Iran and Turan' (به used for domain/region). ورا is the pronominal form 'to him/under him' and bende‑and (بنده‌اند) is plural present/past‑state 'they are servants / they are subject'. The clause states they are subservient to him in those lands.
segments.s012-g40: به رای و به فرمان او uses به instrumentally 'by/through his counsel and by his command' (here به expresses the means). او is 'he', and زنده‌اند is the present plural 'they live / they are kept alive'—used idiomatically to mean they live/continue by his will.
segments.s012-g41: بیاراست is a poetic past third‑person singular of آراستن (‘to adorn’) and here functions as the main verb. روی زمین را is a definite object phrase (روی = face/surface, زمین = earth) marked by را; به داد is an instrumental/means phrase (‘with/through justice’) introduced by به.
segments.s012-g42: بپردخت (classical past 3sg of پرداختن) continues the narrative; here it functions as a past action causally linked to the previous clause. از آن is a causal/relative expression ‘from that / thereupon’, and تاج بر سر نهاد is a compound locational action: نهاد is the past of نهادن (‘placed’), بر سر = ‘on the head’. Word order is Verb after a causal phrase, then the object and locative phrase.
segments.s012-g43: This is a stacked title/name phrase in the order Title (جهاندار) + personal name (محمود) + title (شاه) + adjective (بزرگ). All elements are nominative descriptors forming one nominal subject.
segments.s012-g44: به آبشخور is a directional/prepositional phrase ‘to the watering‑place’ (به indicating motion toward). آرد is a present/habitual 3sg form meaning ‘brings’ or ‘causes to come’; همی is a classical continuous/adverbial particle (still/always) emphasizing habit or repeated action. میش و گرگ are the coordinated nouns (ewe and wolf) that are brought there.
segments.s012-g45: ز ... تا ... marks a geographic range: ز کشمیر ‘from Kashmir’ تا پیش دریای چین ‘as far as before/the shores of the China Sea’. تا here means ‘as far as’, and پیش دریای چین means ‘before / in front of the China Sea’ (i.e., up to its shores).
segments.s012-g46: بر او شهریاران is a prepositional/nominal cluster meaning ‘upon him [the kings/princes]’ where شهریاران is the (plural) subject performing the action. کنند is 3pl present/subjunctive (classical narrative present) ‘they do/say’, and آفرین is the thing they do — an acclamation/praise (‘acclaim’/‘bravo’). The order places the verb کنند before the object/phrase آفرین (typical in classical style).
segments.s012-g47: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when’. کودک is the subject ‘child/infant’; لب از شیر مادر is an object/complement phrase where از indicates source (‘lips from the mother’s milk’ = after nursing); بشست is past 3sg of شستن (‘he/she washed/wiped’), so the whole clause means ‘when the infant wiped its lips of the mother’s milk’. Word order places the object phrase before the verb, as common in classical Persian.
segments.s012-g48: ز گهواره is a prepositional phrase ‘from the cradle’. محمود گوید نخست is a compact clause in which محمود is the name being uttered and گوید (classical 3sg ‘says/utter(s)’) functions impersonally or generally (‘is said/they say’); نخست ‘first’ indicates it is the first name uttered. Note: the provided sense list for گوید does not include the verbal sense ‘he says’, so the correct lexical sense index is not present.
segments.s012-g57: به دل گفتم = a prepositional phrase 'to the heart' (به + دل) followed by the past tense verb گفتم 'I said'; the speaker is addressing his own heart. این خواب را پاسخ است is a clause meaning 'this dream is an answer': را is the (classical/poetic) object/emphasis marker placed after خواب, and است is the copula 'is' finishing the clause.
segments.s012-g58: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the content of the previous statement ('that ...'). آواز او 'his آواز' here means the voice/fame/renown connected to him; بر جهان uses بر 'upon/over' with جهان 'the world', and فرخ است places the predicate adjective 'fortunate' with the copula at the end — a poetic word order meaning 'his voice is auspicious/fortunate upon the world.'
segments.s012-g59: بر آن آفرین 'upon that (thing) a praise' — بر آن marks the object/topic to be praised. کو here is an archaic interrogative 'who/which (one)?' and کند is the 3rd-person present/simple imperfect of کردن 'to do/make', so آفرین کو کند آفرین is an emphatic, poetic question 'who will make (say) the acclamation “afrin”?' (the repetition of آفرین is emphatic).
segments.s012-g60: بر آن begins again 'upon that'; بخت بیدار is a noun phrase 'wakeful/alert fortune' (بخت = fate/destiny, بیدار = awake); و links to فرخ زمین where فرخ modifies زمین 'fortunate/auspicious earth/land.' This is a compact noun phrase praising the fortunate destiny and the fortunate land.
segments.s012-g61: ز فرش جهان 'from the carpet/ground of the world' uses poetic ز = 'from/of' with فرش 'carpet/ground' and جهان 'world'. شد 'became' is the past of شدن, and چو باغ بهار 'like a spring garden' uses چو = 'as/like' followed by the simile phrase; overall: 'the world's ground became like a spring garden.'
segments.s012-g62: هوا پر ز ابر uses the poetic order 'پر ز' (modern پر از) meaning 'full of': 'the air full of clouds.' و links the next phrase زمین پر نگار 'the earth full of ornament/beauties' (نگار here = ornament/beauty/pictures). Note the inverted adjective placement (پر after the noun) typical in poetry.
segments.s012-g63: از ابر 'from the cloud(s)' + اندر آمد 'came in/descended' (اندر آمد is a compound/poetic verb meaning 'came down/into view'). به هنگام نم 'at the time of the dew' places the action in the proper seasonal/time context (هنگام = 'at the time of', نم = 'dew').
segments.s012-g64: جهان شد 'the world became' (شد = became). به کردار is a poetic phrase meaning 'in the manner/appearance of' (کردار here functions as 'way/appearance'), and باغ ارم 'the garden of Iram' (ارم = the legendary garden/paradise). Together: 'the world took on the semblance of the Garden of Iram.'
segments.s012-g25: نشسته is a perfective/adjectival participle used predicatively here = “(was) seated/sat.” بر او gives the location “on it/on him” (poetic anaphor to the throne); شهریاری is the noun ‘sovereign/royal (person)’ placed after the locative phrase, and چو ماه is a simile ‘like the moon’ qualifying the sovereign. Poetic word order (predicate/participle before subject) and the simile immediately follows the noun it modifies.
segments.s012-g26: یکی is an indefinite numeral meaning “one / a certain” (here ‘a single’). تاج is the crown; بر سر is the locative phrase “on (his) head.” به جای is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “in place of / instead of,” and کلاه is the item being replaced (“hat/cap”). Word order: numeral + noun, then the locative, then the substitution phrase.
segments.s012-g27: رده بر کشیده is a verbal phrase: رده (ranks/rows) + بر کشیده (drawn up/arranged), so ‘he drew up the ranks.’ سپاهش = سپاه + enclitic ش (his) = ‘his army.’ دو میل is appended as a measure/feature (two standards/poles). Poetic order places the object/scene before the agent’s possessive phrase.
segments.s012-g28: به دست چپش is the locative phrase ‘in his left hand’ (به + دست + possessive clitic -ش = ‘his’). The numeral هفتصد (seven hundred) quantifies ژنده پیل: ژنده modifies پیل (here an epithet like ‘worn/rough’), and پیل (elephant) is the head noun (plurality expressed by the numeral). The whole phrase lists an impressive element of the host.
segments.s012-g29: یکی پاک دستور: یکی = ‘a certain / one,’ پاک = ‘pure/spotless’ (epithet), دستور here functions as an officer/title (archaic poetic usage, literally ‘order/command’ but used as ‘commander’). پیشش = پیش + clitic = ‘in his presence / before him,’ and به پای = ‘at (his) feet’ (prepositional phrase indicating humility/posture). The noun phrase + locatives describe the attendant’s position relative to the sovereign.
segments.s012-g30: به داد و به دین is a double prepositional phrase ‘in justice and in religion’ (the two spheres). شاه را uses را as the definite/accusative marker marking شاه as the person concerned, and رهنمای is the noun/agent ‘guide/adviser’ placed at the end (verb‑final/nominal final position typical of classical style). Together: ‘(a) guide to the king in matters of justice and religion.’
segments.s012-g31: مرا is the accusative pronoun ‘me’ (here written as م + را in the word list). خیره گشتی is a two-word predicate: خیره = ‘astonished/dazzled,’ گشتی (poetic/past form) gives the action ‘you made / you caused’ or ‘became’ depending on context; together they convey ‘you/it made me astonished’ or ‘I was astonished.’ سر از فر شاه = ‘(my) head from the king’s farr/royal splendour’—از indicates the cause/source, فر (royal glory) names the cause. Poetic word order places the object before the predicate and the causal adjunct after it.
segments.s012-g32: و زان = ‘and from that’ (زان = from that/thereof), linking the previous cause to further causes. ژنده modifies پیلان (an epithet applied to the elephants), and پیلان is the archaic plural of پیل ‘elephants.’ و چندان سپاه = ‘and so many troops/army’ (چندان = ‘so many / to that extent’ modifying سپاه). The whole clause enumerates the things that caused the speaker’s astonishment.
segments.s012-g49: نپیچد is a third-person optative/jussive (classical negative form) meaning roughly “may not / let not (someone) turn.” It stands before کسی (an indefinite/negative scope here: “no one”) to forbid deviation. سر ز فرمان اوی is an idiom: سر (head) + ز (from) + فرمان اوی (his command) = “to turn one’s head away from his command” i.e. to disobey him; اوی is the enclitic possessive “his.”
segments.s012-g50: نیارد here functions in parallel to نپیچد as a negative optative/jussive meaning “may no one …” (i.e. forbid or express that no one will/do). گذشتن is the infinitive/masdar “to pass/go beyond,” and ز پیمان اوی is the prepositional phrase “from his covenant/pact.” Together: “let no one pass beyond his covenant” (i.e. break his oath).
segments.s012-g51: تو نیز آفرین کن is an imperative addressed to “you” (تو): آفرین (acclamation/praise) + کن (do!) = “praise/applaud as well.” که introduces a causal/explanatory clause (“for/because”) and گوینده‌ای is a predicative noun (“a speaker”) describing the subject being praised; the -ای ending is classical/persuasive phrasing (a predicative/indefinite apposition: “for he is a speaker”).
segments.s012-g52: بدو is the prepositional clitic meaning “to him.” نام جاوید is a compound: نام (name/reputation) + جاوید (everlasting) = “an everlasting name/reputation.” جوینده‌ای is placed in apposition (or as the epithet being assigned) — “(the) seeker” — i.e. the phrase assigns/labels him as a seeker. The construction implies: give him an eternal name, (calling him) a seeker.
segments.s012-g53: چو + past-form گشتم = temporal clause “when I became/was” (چو = when). بیدار modifies the state (“awake”). گشتم is a past-tense (first-person) verb “I became/was” and بجستم (past first-person) means “I sprang up / I rose” here; ز جای is the prepositional phrase “from my place.” The whole segment: “When I woke I rose from my place.”
segments.s012-g54: چه مایه is an exclamatory measure phrase “what a quantity/what a weight/what an extent,” here intensifying the following noun. شب تیره = “dark night.” بودم is the past tense “I was,” and به پای (literally “at the foot”) idiomatically indicates the speaker’s standing or the burden underfoot: “I was under / standing in” that dark night. Altogether: “what a (heavy) dark night I was in.”
segments.s012-g55: بر آن شهریار is the prepositional phrase “upon that king/sovereign” (بر = upon, آن = that, شهریار = king). آفرین خواندم uses خواندم (I recited/said) with آفرین (acclamation/praise) as its object: “I recited praise (for) that sovereign.” The verb خواندم is past first-person “I read/recited/sang.”
segments.s012-g56: نبودم درم is an existential/past construction meaning “I had no dirham” (نبودم = I was not/there was not for me; درم = a dirham/coin). جان بر افشاندم is figurative: جان (life/soul) + بر + افشاندم (I scattered/poured forth) = “I poured out my life” or “I gave/spent my life” (i.e. devoted or expended myself). The two halves contrast lack of money with the speaker’s lavish devotion.
segments.s012-g81: ز here is the archaic/poetic preposition 'from/of' marking the following genitive phrase. گیتی پرستنده is an agent noun phrase: پرستنده (present‑participle 'worshipper, devotee') takes گیتی ('the world') as its object; فر و نصر are coordinated objects of admiration (royal glory and victory). The whole phrase is an epithet describing a person.
segments.s012-g82: This is a nominal clause with the name زید followed by the predicative adjective شاد ('happy'). در سایه is a prepositional phrase ('in the shade/protection'); شاه عصر is a compound genitive ('the king of the age/epoch'). The copula ('was') is omitted, as is common in Persian poetic style.
segments.s012-g83: کسی کش is a compound predicative (کسی 'someone/people' + کش as an agentive '‑killer') meaning 'killer of people' or 'manslayer.' In the line's word order the predicate precedes the subject (پدر ناصرالدین); بود is the past copula 'was', linking the subject 'the father of Nāser‑al‑Dīn' to the predicate 'a killer'.
segments.s012-g84: سر تخت او is a locative phrase 'on the head/top of his throne' (سر = top/summit, تخت = throne). تاج پروین بود is a nominal clause with تاج 'crown' and پروین as the identifying name/image; بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause literally states what sat atop his throne.
segments.s012-g85: A simple nominal apposition: و دیگر 'and another' introduces an additional person; دلاور is an epithet 'valiant/brave', and سپهدار طوس is a title + name (سپهدار 'commander' as a title, طوس = Tūs the hero). Word order places adjectives/titles before the name in the customary Persian way.
segments.s012-g86: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause. در جنگ is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in battle.' بر شیر is a prepositional phrase 'against/on the lion' indicating the target of the following nominal object; دارد is the present tense verb 'has/possesses' used here in a habitual/descriptional sense, and فسوس functions as 'idle talk/boast' (i.e. boasting directed at the lion).
segments.s012-g87: ببخشد is the present poetic form of the verb 'to give/bestow' (3rd person). درم is the object 'dirham/coin' used generically as 'money.' هر چه یابد ز دهر is a relative clause 'whatever he finds/obtains from the age/world' (هر چه = 'whatever', یابد = 'he finds/obtains', ز دهر = 'from the world/age'), describing what he gives away.
segments.s012-g88: همی marks continuous or habitual aspect ('constantly/always'). آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' (not the verb 'to create'); یابد is 'finds/receives.' از دهر بهر is a compact, slightly inverted poetic phrase 'from the age/in return' (از دهر = 'from the world/age', بهر = 'for/in return'), saying that the world returns praise to him.
segments.s012-g89: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase identifying God as the one involved; به here functions as 'to/for' that introduces the referent ('God'). بود is the past copula 'was' used to state identity/role, and خلق را رهنمای is a noun phrase 'a guide of the people' with را marking the definite/object-like (emphatic) role of خلق. The whole segment therefore says 'God was/ is the people's guide.'
segments.s012-g90: سر شاه is a genitive-like phrase 'the king's head' (سر as 'chief/leader' in context). خواهد + که introduces a wish/optative ('he/it wishes that…' or 'may it be that…'), and باشد is the subjunctive/capative form of 'to be' meaning 'remain/be (in place)'. به جای is a locative 'in/at its place.' The clause reads as a prayer/optative: 'may the king's head be in its place.'
segments.s012-g91: جهان is the subject 'the world.' بی‌سر is formed with the negative prefix بی‑ 'without' + سر 'head' meaning 'headless' (i.e., leaderless), joined by و to تاج خسرو 'and the crown of the king' (تاج modifies خسرو). مباد is an archaic/poetic negative optative meaning 'may it not be' that governs the whole clause, so the line prays that the world not be without the king's head and crown.
segments.s012-g92: همیشه 'always' is an adverb; بماناد is a third‑person optative/subjunctive 'may (he/it) remain' (poetic form), and جاوید و شاد are predicative adjectives 'eternal and joyful.' Grammatically the optative verb governs the adjectives describing the wished‑for state.
segments.s012-g93: همیشه 'always' modifies the wished state. تن here means 'person/body' (referring to the king), آباد is an adjective 'well/prosperous.' با + تاج و تخت 'with crown and throne' adds accompanying possessions/attributes. The phrase is a blessing: 'may his person be ever well with crown and throne.'
segments.s012-g94: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (here 'free from'). درد و غم lists afflictions 'pain and sorrow.' آزاد is an adjective 'free,' and پیروز بخت is a compound where پیروز 'victorious' qualifies بخت 'fortune/destiny'; together they express being fortunate and triumphant. The whole segment continues the blessing: 'free from pain and sorrow, with victorious fate.'
segments.s012-g95: کنون 'now' sets time; باز 'again' is an adverb. گردم is the first‑person singular verbal form ('I turn/return') used by the narrator to signal returning to an earlier point; به آغاز کار is a prepositional phrase 'to the beginning of the work/tale.' The clause is a narrator's statement 'now I return to the start.'
segments.s012-g96: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward/to.' نامه here means 'book/tale' (not a personal letter) and نامور 'renowned' modifies it. شهریار 'the king/ruler' follows in a classical genitive-like apposition to indicate whose book/tale it is ('the famed book of the king').
segments.s012-g73: The clause begins with the negator نه (neither/not). کند is an auxiliary/performative verb here ('does, makes'); it precedes a sequence of action verbs. آوری (from آوردن) is used in the sense of ‘cause to be brought / exact’ and گیرد (گرفتن) means ‘seize, take.’ The sequence کند آوری گیرد is a poetic stacking of verbs meaning actions he does not perform, and از introduces the sources 'from tribute and treasure.'
segments.s012-g74: The second negated clause again starts with نه. دل تیره is a noun + adjective (‘a dark/gloomy heart’); دارد is the present-tense verb 'has.' The preposition ز (poetic variant of از) marks cause/source: 'from battle and from suffering.' Word order is noun–adjective–verb (دل تیره دارد) common in poetry.
segments.s012-g75: هر آن کس که is a fixed relative phrase 'everyone/anyone who...' که introduces the relative clause. دارد is the present tense 'has' and ز (poetic از) indicates 'among' or 'from' — i.e. 'has among his...'. پروردگان is a plural noun 'those he rears/maintains' (his dependents/servants).
segments.s012-g76: This continues the description of the dependents: از ... و از ... lists two kinds. آزاد here describes people as 'free' (آزادِ مردان) and نیک‌دل is 'kind‑hearted'; بردگان is the head noun 'servants/subjected ones' (in classical usage it can mean servants/slaves but here functions as 'servants'). The repeated از before each adjective is a poetic coordinating device.
segments.s012-g77: شهنشاه را uses را as a topical/definite marker ('as for the king'). سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'entirely/wholly.' دوست‌وار is an adjective 'friend‑like/friendly.' The phrase therefore states the king's whole disposition: 'the king is entirely friendly.'
segments.s012-g78: به فرمان is a circumstantial prepositional phrase 'at/by the (king's) command.' ببسته is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'bound/tied/girded' (an older/poetic form of بسته‌بستن used here attributively), کمر 'waist/loins' and استوار 'steadfast/firm.' Together the clause pictures readiness/firmness: 'his loins are firmly girded at the command.'
segments.s012-g79: نخستین برادرش = 'his first/eldest brother' (نخستین + برادر + the pronominal suffix ش = 'his'). که‌تر به سال is an archaic comparative phrase: -تر is the comparative suffix ('-er'), so که‌تر به سال means 'older by years' or 'more advanced in years.' The clause fragments the description in typical epic, placing the age remark after the noun.
segments.s012-g80: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that.' در مردمی = 'among the people.' کس ندارد = 'has no one' / 'has no person' (ندارد = does not have). همال here means 'peer/equal' (i.e. 'no equal among the people'); the word is used in the sense 'equal' though the provided sense list does not include that meaning. The whole clause says 'who has no equal among the people.'
segments.s012-g65: به ایران is a locative prepositional phrase (به + place) meaning 'in Iran.' همه خوبی is the topical noun phrase 'all (the) goodness.' از داد اوست is a predicative phrase: از = 'from,' داد = 'justice' (noun), and اوست is ου (the pronoun 'he/his') + ست (copula) fused, giving 'is from his justice' (i.e. 'comes from his justice'). Word order is topical fronting + noun phrase + predicate, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s012-g66: کجا هست here functions existentially as 'where there are' (literally 'where is/exist'); مردم همه ('all the people') is the existential subject. یاد اوست is a nominal predicate: یاد = 'remembrance/memory' and اوست = 'of him/it is' (او + ست), so literally 'the remembrance of him is (among them)' → idiomatically 'they all remember him.' The clause uses inverted/poetic word order but follows noun + predicate structure.
segments.s012-g67: به بزم اندرون is a locative phrase: به = 'in/at', بزم = 'banquet/assembly', اندرون = 'inside/within' → 'in the inner banquet-hall.' آسمان is the subject 'the heavens/sky' and سخاست is the predicate; سخاست is an archaic/presentational predicate meaning 'is baseness/disgrace' (the -ست copula is present in form). The order places the locative phrase first for emphasis, then subject + predicate.
segments.s012-g68: به رزم اندرون again gives a locative 'in the midst of battle' (به + رزم + اندرون). تیز چنگ is a compound adjectival phrase: تیز = 'sharp' modifying چنگ = 'claw/talon' (here 'claw'). That adjective phrase immediately precedes the noun/predicate اژدهاست, where اژدها = 'dragon' and -ست is the copula fused, so the clause means 'there is a sharp-clawed dragon' with the adjective placed before the noun for poetic force.
segments.s012-g69: به تن = 'in the body' (به as locative). ژنده پیل is a metaphoric nominal phrase: ژنده = 'ragged' modifying پیل = 'elephant' (i.e. 'a ragged elephant'). The copula 'is' is omitted (typical in Persian poetic style), so the meaning is 'in body (he is) a ragged elephant.' و connects the second parallel phrase; به جان جبرئیل = 'in soul/spirit (he is) Gabriel' (جان = 'soul', جبرئیل = proper name Gabriel). Parallel nominative phrases are linked by و.
segments.s012-g70: به کف ابر = 'on/at the palm of the cloud' (به locative + کف 'palm' + ابر 'cloud'); بهمن follows without an explicit copula and is read as the noun placed there ('Bahman' — here a proper name). به دل = 'in the heart' and رود نیل = 'the Nile river' (رود 'river' + نیل 'Nile') — adjective/noun order is poetic but clear: 'in the heart (is) the Nile river.' The sentence uses a paratactic structure with omitted copulas.
segments.s012-g71: سر بخت is a compound (سر 'head' + بخت 'fate/fortune'); here it forms a noun phrase modified by بدخواه ('ill-wisher' or 'enemy of good fortune') so the whole phrase is roughly 'the (thing/person) of ill-wishing fate.' با خشم = 'with anger' (با + noun), and اوی is an archaic/pronoun form meaning 'his' (possessive/referring back). The clause lacks an explicit copula and is elliptical/poetic.
segments.s012-g72: چو = 'as/like.' دینار is the coin 'dinar.' خوارست appears here as a fused adjectival/predicative form (خوار + ست) meaning something like 'is a devourer/despiser' (the provided lemma is uncertain), so the phrase reads 'is like a dinar-eater.' بر چشم اوی = 'in/ before his eye' (بر = 'on/upon/toward' used idiomatically for 'in his sight'), with اوی again the archaic 'his.' Together: 'as if a dinar‑devourer in his view.'

sentences.s012-s5: He adorned (the realm) from east to west; from his farr a vein of gold appeared.
sentences.s012-s6: My star, which had been asleep, awoke; it turned inward into much thought.
sentences.s012-s7: I knew the time for speech had come; now the ancient age will become new.
sentences.s012-s8: On thoughts of the sovereign of the earth I slept one night, my lips full of praise.
sentences.s012-s1: Until the World‑creator made the world, no border‑guard had yet appeared.
sentences.s012-s2: When the sun showed its crown upon the sky, the earth assumed the guise of shining ivory.
sentences.s012-s3: What can I say of the radiant sun—who he was, from whom light in the world increased.
sentences.s012-s4: Abu al‑Qasim, that victorious king of fortune, set a throne upon the crown of the sun.
sentences.s012-s9: My heart, like a light in that dark night, did not sleep — open of heart and closed of lip.
sentences.s012-s10: Thus my luminous soul saw in sleep that a radiant candle rose up from the water.
sentences.s012-s11: The whole face of the world turned into an ultramarine night; from that candle it became like a yellow ruby.
sentences.s012-s12: Court and plain were like brocade, and a single turquoise throne appeared.
sentences.s012-s17: When I saw that royal face, I asked those renowned men about it.
sentences.s012-s18: Whether this is the sky and the moon, or the crown and an occasion; whether a star is before him or an army.
sentences.s012-s19: One said that this king rules Rome and Hind, from Kannauj as far as the Sea of Sind.
sentences.s012-s20: In Iran and Turan they are his servants; by his counsel and by his command they live.
sentences.s012-s21: He adorned the face of the earth with justice; from that he set the crown upon his head.
sentences.s012-s22: The sovereign Mahmud, the great king, brings both ewe and wolf to the watering‑place.
sentences.s012-s23: From Kashmir to the shores of the Chinese Sea, kings acclaim him.
sentences.s012-s24: When an infant has wiped its lips of its mother's milk, from the cradle the name Mahmud is the first uttered.
sentences.s012-s29: I said to my heart, “This dream is an answer,” — that his voice is fortunate upon the world.
sentences.s012-s30: Who can praise that? Praise be upon that wakeful fortune and the fortunate earth.
sentences.s012-s31: From the world's carpet it became like a spring garden; the air was full of clouds and the earth full of ornament.
sentences.s012-s32: From the clouds came, at the time of the dew; the world became, in appearance, like the garden of Iram.
sentences.s012-s13: Upon it sat a sovereign, like the moon; a single crown on his head in place of a hat.
sentences.s012-s14: He had drawn up the ranks of his army — two standards — and in his left hand (stood) seven hundred battle‑worn elephants.
sentences.s012-s15: A certain pure commander (stood) before him at his feet; a guide to the king in justice and religion.
sentences.s012-s16: You/it stunned me — my head reels from the king's splendour, and from those ragged elephants and so many troops.
sentences.s012-s25: Let no one turn aside from his command; let no one pass beyond his covenant.
sentences.s012-s26: You too give praise; for he is a speaker—give him an everlasting name, a seeker.
sentences.s012-s27: When I awoke I rose from my place; what a weight of dark night I stood in.
sentences.s012-s28: I recited praise upon that sovereign; I had no dirham, yet I poured out my life.
sentences.s012-s41: Zayd, a worshipper of the world's glory and of victory, was happy in the protection (shadow) of the king of the age.
sentences.s012-s42: The father of Nāser‑al‑Dīn was a slayer of men; on the top of his throne was the crown Parvin.
sentences.s012-s43: And another, the brave commander Tūs, who in battle boasts against the lion.
sentences.s012-s44: He gives away every dirham he finds from the age; he continually earns the world's praise in return.
sentences.s012-s45: May God be the guide of the people; may the king's head remain in its place.
sentences.s012-s46: May the world never be headless and without the king's crown; may he remain ever eternal and joyful.
sentences.s012-s47: May his body always be well, with crown and throne; free from pain and sorrow, fortunate and victorious in destiny.
sentences.s012-s48: Now I turn back again to the beginning of the tale, to the famed book of the king.
sentences.s012-s37: He neither exacts nor seizes tribute and treasure; nor does he have a gloomy heart from battle or suffering.
sentences.s012-s38: Every one who has among his dependents free men and kind‑hearted servants.
sentences.s012-s39: The sovereign is wholly friend‑like; at the command his loins are firmly girded (he stands steadfast).
sentences.s012-s40: His first brother — older in years — who among the people has no equal.
sentences.s012-s33: All the goodness in Iran comes from his justice; wherever there are people, they all remember him.
sentences.s012-s34: Within the banquet-hall the heavens show baseness; in the midst of battle there stands a sharp-clawed dragon.
sentences.s012-s35: In body (he is) a ragged elephant, and in soul (he is) Gabriel; on the cloud's palm Bahman, and in the heart the Nile river.
sentences.s012-s36: The ill-wisher's fate-head, with his anger; like a dinar‑devourer he is before his eye.
